data.title: Nine Energy Service: A Play On A Drilling Resurgence
data.description:
Drilling activity for oil and gas has been in severe decline in North America since 2019. This can be seen in the Baker Hughes rig count. Baker Hughes has issued rig counts as a service to the petroleum industry since 1944, when Baker Hughes Tool Company began weekly counts of U.S. and Canadian drilling activity. On 2/15/2019 there were 1,051 U.S. and Canadian rigs in operation. By 4/17/2026, that had fallen to only 673, which was a decline of 46 from the 4/17/2025 count of 719.

There used to be a saying, "Give a Texan a dollar and he'll drill for oil". So far drilling, as measured by the rig count, has not responded to the surge in oil prices shown in Chart I below, as many now consider the surge in oil prices to be transitory. That may not be the case. Recently, in the New York Times, Mark Finley wrote in High Gas Prices Won’t End Even if the War Does

Surprisingly, American oil producers don’t seem to be scrambling to ramp up supply to help offset the Middle East outages. A recent survey by the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas shows that fewer than a quarter of the companies operating in its district plan to significantly increase drilling this year. (Those looking to ramp up drilling are predominantly smaller companies, though.)

I think it is possible that a resurgence of drilling activity in North America could be imminent. There are many companies that could benefit from the increase in oil prices and a subsequent increase in drilling activity. One such company is Nine Energy Service, Inc. (NINE), which has recently emerged from a bankruptcy. NINE suffered from the decline in drilling and a crushing debt burden from its 13% bonds. Those bonds have been converted into almost all of the shares of the reorganized company.

NINE provides cementing services, including blending high-grade cement and water with various solid and liquid additives to create a cement slurry that is pumped between the casing and the wellbore of the well. It also provides open hole and cemented completion tool products, such as liner hangers and accessories, fracture isolation packers, frac sleeves, stage one prep tools, casing flotation tools, specialty open hole float equipment, disk subs, composite cement retainers, and centralizers that provide pinpoint frac sleeve system technologies.

NINE is highly leveraged to drilling activity in North America, which in turn ultimately should depend on the prices for oil and gas. While NINE is risky, it does not have some of the risk aspects that some other plays on higher oil prices could have. Oil and natural gas producers would benefit from higher prices but could be vulnerable to windfall profits taxes and/or price controls. Suppliers of oilfield equipment and services like NINE would generally not be vulnerable to windfall profits taxes on producers and/or price controls. NINE operates primarily in North America. Thus, it has not suffered from the war as the major international oilfield equipment firms have and may continue to do so on an ongoing basis. For example, the war has prompted SLB to announce that as a result of the war, “SLB revenue for the first quarter will be lower than expected, and the company expects to incur additional costs resulting in an impact of approximately 6-9 cents of earnings per diluted share for the first quarter.”

International companies and those listed primarily on foreign stock exchanges could be hurt if the war and/or the surge in world oil and natural gas prices reduces economic activity and investor sentiment. A prolonged increase in oil and natural gas prices could hurt the US equity markets less than many others, as the US is a net energy exporter.

There are other developments that might augur an increase in drilling. On April 16, 2026, the Trump administration urged the oil industry to increase production during a videoconference with senior executives from major operators, including Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), and Continental Resources, as well as independent producers such as Diamondback Energy (FANG), Devon Energy (DVN), and Occidental Petroleum (OXY). One possible action that the Trump administration could do to increase drilling activity would be to reduce tariffs on steel pipe used in drilling for oil and gas. In the most recent Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas survey of the oil and gas industry, several comments highlighted steel tariffs (25 percent, levied under Section 232) specifically as driving higher costs and making expanding production less appealing.

To some extent, oil- and gas-related stocks are a binary bet. Either prices will remain elevated for a prolonged period, or they will not. These two scenarios can be categorized as either the "high price" or "low price" scenarios. Without going into the debate surrounding the war with Iran, there are a few possible outcomes that could result in the high-price scenario. These include the conflict causing much more destruction to oil and gas facilities in the Middle East than has occurred so far. This could happen if no agreement between Iran and the US is reached, and the US carries out the threat to destroy Iran’s energy infrastructure. Iran would likely respond by destroying much of the energy infrastructure of the other countries in the region. That would also be Iran’s likely response to an American ground invasion. This would likely result in much higher oil and LNG prices for an extended period of time.

Another scenario that could result in the high-price scenario would be some type of stalemate that results in continued blockage of the Strait of Hormuz and possibly other routes as well. Maj. Gen. Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi said Iran's armed forces would block Persian Gulf, Sea of Oman, and Red Sea traffic in response to the US naval blockade. Iran would not need a navy to prevent vulnerable oil tankers and LNG carriers from using the Strait of Hormuz, the Persian Gulf, the Sea of Oman, and the Red Sea, as they all would be within range of Iran’s drones and ballistic missiles. The US could also block all maritime traffic to and from Iran for as long as it wanted to.

The high price scenario could also result from some combination of destruction to oil and gas facilities in the Middle East and interference with marine navigation. In the high-price scenario, increased oil and gas production from the Western Hemisphere, particularly North America, replaces much of the oil and LNG previously going to Asian customers. Even without extensive disruption of future oil and LNG supplied from the Middle East, Asian buyers could turn to oil and gas production from the Western Hemisphere if they were seeking more reliability.

In the US there has been a divergence between how domestic oil and natural gas prices responded to the war with Iran. The dramatic increase in domestic oil prices, shown in the WTI chart below, has not been replicated with domestic natural gas prices. That is because of capacity constraints that now limit how much LNG can be exported from the US. That situation will be changing even if the problems associated with the conflict in the Middle East were solved; US exports of LNG will surge in the next few years. In 2025, US LNG exports were about 15.0 to 15.1 Bcf/d, equaling 13% of total US production. As new LNG terminals come on stream in 2026 and 2027, US exports will grow by 30%, and another increase in capacity after that will bring LNG exports of US LNG to 26 to 30 Bcf/d. This increase will benefit companies involved in producing and transporting US natural gas.

It is possible that if LNG infrastructure in the US increases enough, natural gas pricing in the US could rise to world prices, as is the case for oil now. That could bring about a situation where the "low price" scenario applies to US oil prices, but the "high price" scenario applies to US natural gas prices. In earnings calls and presentations, NINE management has said that they believed their business was slightly more sensitive to US natural gas prices relative to oil prices and was extremely sensitive to both.

In OILK: An Energy Crunch In The Making - Needlessly I discussed how insistence from the financial markets that firms in the oil industry emphasize capital discipline versus an historical emphasis on reserves accumulation was setting up a situation where the inelasticity of both supply and demand for oil could result in higher prices even as demand declines. The models discussed in that article would project a tremendous increase in capital spending in the oil industry if the current high levels of oil prices were to persist. NINE would likely have much higher earnings in that case.

It is fair to say that the majority opinion of financial market participants now is that the current high levels of oil prices are transitory. A colleague of mine at White Weld, John Mendelson, was fond of saying that the purpose of financial markets is to punish the majority opinion. Thus, it can be said that the "low price" scenario is now in the market. This would make NINE and, to a lesser extent, most domestic oil and gas-related securities a contrarian play.

In the low-price scenario, NINE would suffer along with most domestic oil- and gas-related securities. NINE has only 14 million shares outstanding. Their pro forma statement filed on February 1, 2026, prior to the surge in oil prices, projects net income in FY28 of $16,000,000, which would represent the low-price scenario. As shown in Table I below, the profits that NINE generates in FY28 are primarily due to the reduction in net interest expense to only $3 million as compared to $55 million in 2025. In Will Oil Production Fall Off A Cliff?, I examined the outlook for oil- and gas-related industry capital spending. Spending was modeled as a function of past and projected levels of the price of West Texas Intermediate crude as determined by the crude oil futures market. The model would project a very significant increase in the rig count were oil prices projected by the crude oil futures market to remain at these levels.

To summarize, the possible resurgence of drilling activity in North America has the potential to greatly benefit domestic energy companies. NINE is a high-risk situation that could bring significant gains. Diversification is always a good idea. In addition to NINE, oil and gas-related stocks that I believe could benefit from the prolonged increase in oil and gas prices and subsequent increase in drilling that I own are Energy Transfer (ET), Harvest Oil & Gas (HRST), SandRidge Energy (SD), UBS ETRACS Crude Oil Shares Covered Call ETN (USOI), and Petrobras (PBR) (PBR.A).

data.title: I Am Still Buying Lumentum, But For A Better Reason Now
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my previous work on Lumentum Holdings (LITE), I leaned on AI-driven demand and improvement in its profitability. I also talked about the optical circuit switches [OCS] backlog going above $400Mn, incremental CPO orders, and the NVIDIA (NVDA) relationship. I also believe that I made the valuation argument in the right way. Because since then, LITE's stock price has increased by 45% as of writing this. And it's not that the stock was cheap on valuation headlines, as I will demonstrate in the later sections.

I also said that Lumentum was "selling a product into a real bottleneck in AI infrastructure." And that line still holds water in my view. I believe that what has changed since is where the next layer of upside sits. And in this regard, I believe that LITE is moving from being a scarce component supplier to becoming a higher-content optical platform provider. That bridge is the external light source (ELS) architecture. I am saying this because in the Q2 2026 earnings call, management highlighted that it had a line of sight to participate more 'holistically,' as noted by the CEO, Michael Hurlston:

...we have established a clear line of sight into the broader external light source, or ELS market, which would enable us to participate more holistically than as a stand-alone laser chip supplier...

Now why is this important? Because during the Morgan Stanley Tech, Media & Telecom conference, the CEO went on to add an important update on the external light source. It is expected to lift revenue content by about 2x to 2.5x relative to selling only lasers. LITE's own product materials describe ELSFP modules as centralized, serviceable light sources for CPO systems that improve thermal management serviceability and scalability.

As a result, I am reiterating Lumentum with a Strong Buy rating. My previous rating was based on LITE mostly shipping more EMLs, more transceivers, and more OCS into AI clusters. But my reiteration comes from the fact that now there is a chance to capture more dollars per deployment as systems shift toward pluggable external light sources and later broader optical scale-up links. A report from McKinsey & Company shows that production of 800G transceivers could remain between 40% and 60% short of demand through 2027. On the other side, 1.6T shortfalls could persist through 2029. In my opinion, if optics stay supply constrained, the winners won't just be companies with a demand story. But also, they'll be the ones with product breadth and the ability to add capacity. And LITE is positioning itself to be one of them.

In Q2 2026, the company highlighted that optical scale was becoming a growth driver. I also note that LITE noted that the first scale-up CPO shipments are expected in late 2027. At OFC 2026, LITE showed a VCSEL-based optical scale-up solution that is aimed at rack-level architectures using UCle and PCle-style protocols. Remember, the 1060nm VCSEL platform is built on a 3D sensing manufacturing base that has already shipped more than 10Bn emitters. I also note that VCSELs will be undersupplied through 2029 based on McKinsey estimates.

There is also a stronger supply-side moat now than the market a year ago. I am saying this because in February, management said that its indium phosphide fabs were fully allocated. And that it was still undershipping customer demand by roughly 25% to 30%. At the Morgan Stanley (MS) Conference, the CEO noted that LITE had just struck a 7-year substrate supply agreement. Then there is also the NVDA partnership, which includes a multi-billion-dollar purchase commitment and a $2Bn investment to support R&D and US manufacturing. I would also like to mention the Greensboro fab deal, which is expected to add a 240k square-foot US site for CW and UHP InP devices. The production is expected to ramp up in mid-2028. In my view, all these are not just demand stories anymore. But also I would say they are also controlled supply stories too.

Upcoming Earnings Release

Lumentum is expected to report their Q3 2026 on May 5th, 2026. The first thing I'd listen for is the OCS conversion. The company had highlighted that it had moved well past $400Mn with most of it expected to ship in H2 2026. In my view, a strong indication would indicate its readiness in manufacturing while still moving ahead off plan. Secondly, I will be keen on transceiver quality. And not just the transceiver volume. LITE had noted that 1.6T demand is ramping faster than it expected. This means that early 1.6T demand is leaning heavily into 200G EMLS, and 1.6T margins are meaningfully better than 800G. Third, I will also be keen on ELS and scale up. This implies that any indications around customer sampling progress, better visibility into the ELSFP module adoption, show that LITE is winning beyond pure laser content.

Valuation

When I look at the valuation multiples, I note a couple of things. First and foremost, the company scores a D+ valuation grade. LITE trades at an FWD non-GAAP P/E of 114.84x, while the sector median stands at 23.84x. This is quite a rich premium of 385%. Additionally, the FWD EV/EBITDA stands at 71.30x, while the sector median stands at 14.11x. Again, quite a rich premium of 405% relative to the sector median.

From a first look, I would say that Lumentum is trading at a high premium relative to its peers and sector medians. But again, I remember this is an AI infrastructure company that is priced faster in a broader role than many investors still think it has. My point is this: it is expensive, yes. But not expensive in a vacuum. And this is why, when I look at LITE from a growth-adjusted perspective, the story changes. The below growth profile shows that Lumentum's revenue growth rate y/y stood at 48.89%, while the sector median stood at 9.87%. Additionally, the FWD revenue growth rate sits at 54.16%, while the sector median is at 10.73%. If I also look at the FWD EPS long-term growth rate, LITE stands at 132.11%, while the sector median sits at 16.20%.

Now, when I combine growth and [EPS], I arrive at the PEG ratio, which I believe gives a better and clearer indication of where Lumentum should stand. And this is true because LITE trades at an FWD Non-GAAP PEG of 0.87x, while the sector median trades at 1.45x. This suggests that LITE is discounted by 40.04% relative to the sector median. What is interesting is that since my initial coverage, the multiple discounts have actually expanded. Because since the last piece, the stock was trading at an FWD non-GAAP PEG of 0.88x, while the sector median stood at 1.27x, suggesting a discount of 30.51%.

When I look at the peers, I note that LITE is trading at an attractive FWD non-GAAP PEG. Coherent Corp. (COHR) still scores an F valuation grade. But its FWD non-GAAP PEG stands at 1.69x. On the other side, Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) also scores an F valuation grade. Its FWD non-GAAP PEG sits at 2.54x, which is still higher than the sector median. And lastly, we have Fabrinet (FN), which also scores a D valuation grade.

Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for Lumentum at the moment is a timing mismatch. And I am saying this because LITE is broadening content, adding capacity, and pushing into scale-up while the market is already capitalizing on a lot of that upside. This means that if scale-up slips, ELS adoption is slower than expected, or if hyperscaler architectures migrate faster than the market expects, the stock could get punished quickly. The second risk is customer concentration and execution risk around fab ramps. In the previous piece, I still highlighted customer concentration as a risk. As of now, that is still a key risk, given that LITE shipped to 3 key customers in Q2 2026.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, Lumentum is a great company. And I believe it warrants a Strong Buy rating. I am no longer leaning only on the obvious things like AI optics demand, OCS backlog, or the NVDA deal. This time, I am leaning more on the idea that LITE is becoming one of the companies that hyperscalers and compute vendors increasingly need to solve the optical capacity problem and the copper wall at the same time. And this is why I am reiterating Lumentum Holdings with a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Commodities: Peace Talk Optimism Clouds Reality
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy: Oil trading on hopes rather than reality

While energy markets popped higher yesterday following Iran’s decision to reverse its opening of the Strait of Hormuz, they’re still trading in a manner that suggests optimism over US-Iran talks. The aim, of course, is to establish a viable off-ramp that enables energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz to resume on a sustained, long-term basis. But we believe markets are underpricing the ongoing supply disruption. Optimism appears to be clouding the reality of the supply shock.

Negotiations between the US and Iran are set to resume in Pakistan, with US Vice President JD Vance set to attend. It appears Iran will send a delegation too. This follows earlier suggestions that Iran wouldn’t attend as long as the US blockade continues.

These talks are important, with the current ceasefire set to end on Wednesday. President Trump has suggested he is unlikely to extend the ceasefire. Therefore, a lack of progress would likely push oil and gas prices higher. This would create significant uncertainty over when energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz might return to normal.

The longer these supply disruptions persist, the tighter the oil market becomes, leaving a longer path towards normalisation for markets once hostilities end. Energy flows will take time to recover. Upstream production will also take time.

We also need to see restocking globally following significant stock drawdowns. Taking these factors into account - along with the likelihood that any US–Iran agreement would remain fragile - it appears that while oil prices would face downside pressure, the market’s floor for the rest of the year is considerably higher than it was before the war.

The European gas market is also becoming more optimistic about the prospect of a peace deal. TTF is currently trading below EUR40/MWh. These are basically the levels we saw in late January amid the deep freeze across the US, when there was plenty of concern over US LNG export supply. The current disruption in Persian Gulf LNG supplies is clearly more impactful for the market, yet prices don’t appear to fully reflect this.

For the European gas market, LNG send-outs remain seasonally strong, while gas storage has broken back above 30% full. This will offer some comfort to the market. Moreover, global gas demand is set to weaken, with the power sector switching from gas to coal, easing some of the upward pressure on prices.

In terms of LNG supply, the market is tight. Golden Pass LNG in the US is set to imminently load its first LNG cargo from train 1, but with this train having an annual capacity of around 6 mtpa, it is far short of the 77 mtpa of Qatari LNG capacity currently disrupted.

Metals: Gold softer on renewed inflation concerns

Gold started the week on a weaker footing as higher oil and gas prices reignited inflation concerns. Ongoing disruptions risk around the Strait of Hormuz are keeping energy markets supported, reinforcing inflation expectations and acting as a near-term headwind for gold.

We see this capping upside in the short term, though downside risks appear limited. Elevated geopolitical tensions and continued uncertainty around Hormuz should underpin haven demand.

Attention now shifts to Tuesday’s US Senate confirmation hearing for Kevin Warsh, nominee to chair the Federal Reserve. Any hawkish signals could add further pressure on gold. While gold prices have recovered some conflict-driven losses, gold still trades around 8% below pre-conflict levels following earlier liquidity-driven selling.

Near-term price action is likely to remain volatile amid shifting expectations around central bank policy, real yields and persistent geopolitical uncertainty.

In other precious metals, China’s silver imports surged to a record high in March, boosted by demand from retail investors and the solar sector. Imports rose to around 836 tonnes, according to Chinese customs data, well above the 10‑year March average of roughly 306 tonnes.

Earlier in the year, strong domestic demand pushed Chinese silver prices to a premium over international markets, triggering arbitrage flows. Silver prices have since then pulled back from their January record highs, while retail momentum has softened.

In base metals, copper edged lower after recent gains but continues to trade near two-month highs, while macro headwinds are slowing down momentum. Higher energy prices and sticky inflation risk keeping monetary policy tighter for longer, weighing on the industrial metals demand outlook.

Aluminium prices remain supported by ongoing supply disruptions in the Middle East. Production in Gulf countries dropped 6% to 15,963 tonnes a day in March, according to data from the International Aluminium Institute.

Agriculture: India allows further wheat exports

India has approved an additional 2.5mt of wheat exports, according to the Ministry of Consumer Affairs and Food. The aim is to support farm incomes, manage domestic inventories, and limit distress sales during the peak marketing season.

This raises total permitted wheat exports to 5mt in the current year. Meanwhile, wheat acreage is expected to increase by 2% year-on-year to 33.42m hectares, despite fertiliser supply constraints. Wheat production for 2026 is projected at 120.2mt.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: ValuEngine Weekly Market Summary And Commentary
data.description:
Weekly Market Recap – Week Ending April 17, 2026

U.S. equity markets extended their upward momentum this week, led by strong gains in technology and growth-oriented segments, as reflected in the sharp advances in the Nasdaq-100 and semiconductor-linked names.

Broad-based indices also moved higher, supported by resilience in large caps and selective strength across communication services and consumer discretionary sectors. However, performance remained uneven beneath the surface, with energy and certain defensive sectors lagging, indicating a continued rotation rather than a uniform rally.

Over the past month, the leadership in high-beta and innovation-driven stocks has been particularly pronounced, suggesting improving risk appetite, though sector divergences highlight that investors are still navigating a mixed macro backdrop.

In the below tables we use major ETFs as a proxy for some major indexes as well as each of the sector groups into which we divide the overall markets. Tracking these over time provides a more defined picture of the US markets than simply tracking major indexes. This is followed by notable individual stock movers over the past month and, finally, our full strategy outlook.

Strategy Note

Overvaluation is Back – Will “AI Stocks” Be the Main Target Again?

Wall Street has referred to last week’s rally as the “war trade unwind,” with the S&P 500 entering the week at record highs. It has surged more than 13% in the past three weeks. Winners well outpaced losers. The energy sector was the major exception, with the StateStreet Select Energy Sector SPDR (XLE) down 4.4%.

The inflation issue is not going away any time soon. Even if the Iran crisis is mollified, not a given in the wake of major static in the weekend’s negotiations, recent numbers have been going in the wrong direction. March CPI saw its largest monthly gain since 2022.

Beyond resolving geopolitical issues, the main fear in the stock market looking at the week ahead is overvaluation. The Chartered Market Technicians Association (CMT) estimates that SPY, StateStreet’s standard-bearer ETF Trust for the S&P 500, has transitioned from oversold to overbought in just 12 days.

By our proprietary valuation model, 60% of the stocks ValuEngine covers are now overvalued as compared with 52% just three weeks ago. It is using this lens that portfolio managers will use their tools to evaluate this week’s earnings announcements as we head into peak season.

93 S&P 500 companies will report in the upcoming week. The largest corporate names include the big four of defense: GE (GE), RTX Corp. (formerly Raytheon) (RTX), Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Boeing (BA). RTX is the highest rated of the group with a 5 (Strong Buy) for price growth over the next 1-12 months.

After that, GE is rated 4 (Buy) while LMT and BA are both rated 3 (hold). The list of other corporate giants reporting includes IBM (IBM), Tesla (TSLA). American Express (AXP), AT& T (T) and Procter & Gamble (PG). All five stocks are rated 3 (Hold).

When it comes to the overvaluation issue, TSLA figures to be in the spotlight again as the lone “Magnificent Seven” stock reporting this week. These stocks, considered by many to be overvalued, have been targeted during the prior 9 months or so as sources of funds for value and small-cap stocks.

These stocks are also associated with the “AI boom,” which many analysts feel has gotten far ahead of itself. Some have gone as far as to assert we are in a new tech bubble. We are not so sure, so let’s take a look.

Of the Mag 7 stocks, only Nvidia (NVDA) and Google (GOOGL) are rated 5 (Strong Buy) while Amazon (AMZN) is rated 4 (Buy). While AMZN is 10% overvalued and GOOGL is 40% overvalued by our valuation model, NVDA is actually rated 16% undervalued by our model’s metrics.

Those three stocks, along with Meta Platforms (META) are probably the most involved with actual AI prototypes, model building, testing, and related activities. META, however, is rated 3 (Hold). So are the remaining stocks in the group, Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Netflix (NFLX).

Reverting to the stocks that are actually reporting this week, there are a few somewhat smaller stocks that get our highest rating of 5 along with NVDA and GOOGL. They are chipmaker Intel (INTC), Lam Research (LRCX) and Vertiv Holdings (VRT).

We find LCRX especially interesting in its AI involvement. It is a “foundational” AI play because it provides the physical tools required to manufacture the hardware that runs AI models. The Silicon Valley-based firm is a leader in enabling high bandwidth memory (HBM), intrinsic to 3D stacking where memory layers are stacked on top of each other.

Beyond that, Lam is a leader in manufacturing next-generation “Angstrom-Era” chips with atomic layer disposition and Etch technologies that facilitate customers’ (e.g., Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM)) ability to sculpt transistors at the atomic level.

What is more, their “ALTUS Halo” tool uses molybdenum, a metal that reduces resistance in nano-scale wires by 50%, enabling the faster processing speeds AI requires.

There is a lot more to the story of their leadership in these aspects of the industry that may be worth our readers’ time in checking them out as a company, even if they find the stock too overvalued. Indeed, our valuation model ranks it in the most overvalued 10% of our coverage universe.

Ohio-based Vertiv is also heavily involved, serving as a critical infrastructure partner for AI data centers by providing advanced cooling and power management technologies. The firm collaborates with leaders like NVDA and INTC to support high-density AI computing, offering solutions such as liquid cooling for accelerating AI deployments.

All four of these companies are expected by our model to gain more than 15%, with VRT forecast to gain 34% in the next 12 months. Once again, the company’s intrinsic importance to AI advancements makes it susceptible to huge overvaluation.

Like LCRX, VRT is in the most overvalued 10%, while Intel’s overvaluation is even greater, ranking it in the top 4%. The conclusion is that if you believe in using our models in tandem as ValuEngine Capital Management LLC does, NVDA is the bargain of the group to get top forecast price appreciation at a below-average valuation.

It’s also the only one of the quartet not reporting this week, as that report is scheduled for May. Regardless, for price growth, all four are probably worth some research, as they are clear leaders in at least one aspect of AI.

Going back to general market overvaluation, whether this will be the week that overvaluation wreaks havoc on some major market stocks is a question we cannot answer. For now, we focus on the forecast earnings growth leaders with a sharp eye on the balances between expected return and anticipated price volatility.

data.title: Laughing Water Capital Q1 2026 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners,

During the first quarter of 2026 a Class A investment in Laughing Water Capital returned approximately -4.5% net of all fees and expenses. i During the quarter, the SP500TR and R2000 returned -4.3% and 0.9% respectively. As always, please check your individual statements for the most accurate measure of your individual return, which may vary based on fund, class, and timing. As a reminder, our portfolio is completely different than the indexes, so there is no reason for us to think that our performance will be similar to the indexes over shorter periods of time. However, over longer periods of time I expect we will be rewarded by taking the road less travelled. On this front, during the quarter Laughing Water Capital passed the ten year mark since inception. Over the first ten years of Laughing Water's existence, returns were approximately 410%, or 17.7% per year, versus 339% or 15.9% annualized for the SP500TR and 189.2% or 11.2% annualized for our most relevant benchmark, the R2000. ii

Despite this past success, the future is of course what matters. Of particular note, at present our portfolio is more concentrated than it has been historically, meaning the short-term fluctuations are likely to be more extreme. The reason for this increased concentration is increased confidence in the near and intermediate term prospects of several of our holdings. Each of these holdings trades day to day based on the vagaries of the market and the quantitative read of their financial statements. However, I believe each of them has value far in excess of what a cursory review would suggest, and I believe this value will be revealed shortly through a series of idiosyncratic events that should take place regardless of what else is happening in the world. I believe that bearing the immediate term volatility will be worth it for the intermediate term reward.

I would also note that this past quarter has been eventful for the composition of our portfolio as several longer held positions have been reduced or exited, while several new positions have taken their place. Some part of these changes are tied to negative developments in legacy positions, but the over-riding influence has simply been finding better opportunities. In this case "better" means stronger balance sheets, better competitive positions, more predictable event paths, less reliance on macro or industry forces, and at times cheaper valuations. I believe the next ten years for Laughing Water Capital can be as successful as the last ten years, and for this reason almost the entirety of my and my family's wealth remains invested right alongside yours. Our interests are aligned.

Where Are We Now?

“We didn’t start the fire - It was always burning since the world’s been turning”

Events of the last three months including advancements in artificial intelligence, U.S. interventions in Venezuela and Iran, and resultant rising oil prices would fit perfectly as a verse in Billy Joel’s classic song that recounts the major events of much of the second half of the 20 th century. I think it is important however to remember that the point of the song is that crazy things have always been happening, and the world just keeps on spinning. For us, this means that we should mostly ignore the global-macro-political noise, and instead stay focused on the prospects of our individual companies.

That being said, volatility creates opportunities, and there have been several changes to our portfolio in recent months. Most notably, over the last few years much of our portfolio has been in names where the thesis was often multi-year in nature. Further, value was not always apparent without significant diligence, and longer timelines meant that ultimate value was subject to increased event path risk.

In contrast, the latest additions to our portfolio are characterized by shorter timelines, value that is underpinned by rock solid balance sheets and near term cash flows, and limited event path risk. I will share more thoughts on this evolution in future communications, but in many ways this is simply me leaning into what has worked better for us in the past, and leaning away from the type of investments that have powered much of the markets returns in recent years.

New Positions

Undisclosed – We have a new investment in our Top 5 that will remain undisclosed for now as I plan to increase the position size. In brief, this investment is in a “good co / bad co” situation, where one company operates two segments, one of which is very attractive, and one of which is less attractive. The good co has a dominant competitive position and generates a lot of cash. The combined company has an unlevered balance sheet and trades at ~10x my estimate of normalized FCF. Recent changes to the board and management, as well as the arrival of an activist, suggest that the good co will be allowed to shine in the not too distant future. Please stay tuned.

Stride Inc. (LRN) -Stride entered our portfolio as a mid-sized position. The company is the largest operator of virtual schools for grades K-12 in the U.S., serving more than 240,000 students across 30 states. Virtual schools are enjoying secular tail winds as advances in technology (Zoom, Google Meet etc.) and changes to the way online education is perceived following the Covid experience have led to what I believe is a sustainable increase in demand.

The company had been growing at mid to low teens percent for several years and traded at ~14x EBIT when they guided to 10-15% growth in enrollment for fiscal '26. However, prior to the 2025-2026 school year the company attempted to upgrade the software that governs their enrollment process as well as their learning management software. The implementation did not go smoothly, and in late October of 2025 management announced they would be cutting guidance. In brief, the new software was disjointed to the point that parents could not successfully navigate the enrollment process, and if successful, students faced problems with logging in and finding the right classes and curriculum. This cost the company upwards of 10,000 enrollments and led to a more than 50% decline in share price, where we began to buy stock. In response to these events, management initiated a $500M buyback and moved quickly to fix the software problems.

Given that commentary from both Stride and a key competitor indicates that demand for virtual schools remains strong, this investment logically comes down to one issue; either they can fix their software, or they cannot. At present, the company is valued below 7.5x EBIT, which seems to imply either 1) that the company will not be able to fix their software or 2) that the market does not have the patience to wait for the company to fix their software. I am of the view that given enough time, the problems will be fixed, and in fact early indications from both the company and various open market sources are that the company is well on its way down this path. As the fixes take hold, I expect shares to re-rate closer to where they traded prior to the sell off.

It is also important to note that unlike many for profit education companies that have left a sour taste in Wall Street's mouth in the past, Stride is NOT reliant on students taking on loans that they cannot afford in order to attend school. Rather, Stride enters into long term contracts with school districts who fund the students' education.

Theravance Biopharma, Inc. (TBPH) -In American football, if the defense goes offsides but the play still starts, the offense has a tremendous advantage. If they execute a successful play, the play stands. If they are unsuccessful, they are not penalized for the attempt due to the offsides call. The offense is thus incentivized to go for the end zone.

Theravance is a special situation that fits this framework. A longer write up can be found at the end of this letter, but in brief, following a failed Phase III FDA trial the company is a pile of cash and similar attached to a royalty stream that should generate ~$65M per year for the Company, as well as $2.6B in tax assets. The company has announced that they have accelerated the work of a strategic review committee, essentially saying that the royalty stream is for sale. It is hard to know exactly what a buyer will pay, but the downside is well protected by cash and near cash, and anything from $17-$25 is well within reason. Under certain scenarios, a buyer may be willing to pay a few dollars more. We bought our shares below $14, and in my view, the downside protection and range of upside outcomes is such that we should go for the end zone in terms of position sizing. Theravance is thus a top 5 position for us. I expect this situation to resolve itself within the next 0-6 months.

Also of note:

Nextnav Inc (NN) – I have written about Nextnav, our wireless spectrum / 5G terrestrial backup to GPS investment, several times in the past. This investment remains an outsized position, and it remains subject to wild swings every month around the timing of the release of each month's Federal Communications Commission (FCC) agenda. The swings can be frustrating, but in my view, the investment has been largely de-risked as the FCC submitted a draft Notice of Proposed Rule Making (NPRM) to the White House Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (OIRA).

In other words, it seems that the FCC is now on our side.

However, the proposal still needs to pass OIRA review, which would entail checking with other agencies that could be affected by the proposal. In this case, that would primarily be the Department of Transportation (DOT) and the Department of Defense (DOD). From my perspective, it seems unlikely that FCC Chair Brendan Carr would submit a proposal to OIRA without first running it by DOT and DOD. Further, the DOT and FCC both fall under oversight from the Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee, which is chaired by Senator Ted Cruz. Cruz has been a vocal advocate for terrestrial GPS in the past, which seems to indicate that the DOT will be on board.

The DOD's spectrum usage is overseen by the National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA), The NTIA is part of the Department of Commerce under Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick. Lutnick in the past has said that the government has too much spectrum, and it should be freed up for better use cases. Additionally, a backup to GPS is indeed something that the DOD wants and needs.

Lastly, recall that it was Trump who started the whole push for a backup to GPS/PNT with an executive order at the end of his first term, so it seems unlikely that an arm of the executive branch would now torpedo Nextnav's proposal.

The timing here has admittedly stretched a few months longer than I hoped for, but I think we are almost there, and I expect to see an NPRM shortly. Following an NPRM there would be a comment period that could stretch to 60 days from the time the NPRM is posted in the Federal Registrar. At this point much of what could be said has already been said during the Notice of Inquiry phase of this preceding, so I am hopeful comments will move quickly. From there I would expect a Report and Order which will allow Nextnav to use their spectrum for 5G communications and a terrestrial backup to GPS.

To be clear, we still do not know what will be in an NPRM, and it is possible that Nextnav will not get everything they have asked for, which would of course weigh on the value of the spectrum. However, the entire point of this exercise is to incentivize the mobile network operators (VZ, T, TMUS) or other interested parties to roll out a terrestrial backup to GPS nationwide. In order to properly motivate them, the FCC must ensure that the spectrum in question is attractively configured. If the spectrum is attractively configured, it should be quite valuable.

“How valuable” is an open question, but recent transactions suggest something north of $50 (vs. month end price of ~$15) is well within range. It is also worth noting that there is a history of spectrum pricing exceeding the top end of expectations during competitive bid processes. Recent news that Amazon (AMZN) is interested in terrestrial spectrum through Globalstar (GSAT) and that SpaceX is looking to acquire more spectrum can only be viewed as positive. Afterall, all that is needed to push value higher in an auction process of a scarce asset is two interested buyers. The arrival of two likely bidders with very deep pockets could push the price significantly higher.

Liquidia Corp (LQDA) -Liquidia is our maker of a drug called YUTREPIA that combats Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) and Pulmonary Hypertension Interstitial Lung Disease (PH-ILD). The sales launch of this drug continues to exceed all expectations, and the stock has appreciated nicely. However, I believe shares would be much higher if not for an on-going patent battle with United Therapeutics (UTHR). Legal arguments in the last round concluded last summer, but the judge has not yet ruled. My work around the opinions of legal experts and past rulings in similar cases by this judge suggest a favorable outcome is on the way. The delay is of course frustrating, but I think the likely explanation is that the judge is taking the time to write an opinion that would be bullet proof on appeal. Logically, if he were going to rule against Liquidia he would do so quickly, because every day that he has not ruled is another day that more patients are put on YUTREPIA, and historically judges have not wanted to pull medication from people whose lives are improved by it.

Importantly, this trial is only tied to the use of YUTREPIA to treat PH-ILD. In other words, even if the ruling is unfavorable Liquidia should be able to continue to take share in the PAH market. This should provide downside protection for the stock. If the ruling is favorable and the launch of YUTREPIA continues unimpeded, even a material slow down in the rate of adoption since inception could lead to a revenue run rate north of $1.2B by the end of the year. If Liquidia were to trade at the same multiple of sales as United Therapeutics, shares would be worth more than double where they are today. Additionally, the major pharmaceutical companies are facing an estimated $300B in patent cliffs over the next few years. These same companies have an estimated $1T in dry powder to make acquisitions in order to fill in the looming patent hole. Liquidia appears to be a prime candidate for a takeout by large pharma.

SECURE Waste Infrastructure Corp (SES-TO) – SECURE was introduced in our 1H'25 letter as a business that had transitioned from oil services to waste services tied to the Canadian oil industry. This transition, combined with wonky accounting, explained why shares were cheap, and we purchased our shares just after an undersubscribed tender offer was completed for around C$14.50. As I was about to hit “send” on this letter, news surfaced that GFL Environmental Inc. (GFL), a Canadian-American waste player that is ~6x larger than SECURE, would be purchasing SECURE for C$24.75 per share in a stock and cash deal. My initial reaction is to not be thrilled with this price.

Lifecore Biomedical Inc. (LFCR) -Lifecore, our fill finish CDMO that puts drugs into syringes and vials, continues to win new business at an impressive rate. However, the time from winning new business to generating meaningful cash flows from that business is measured in years in the CDMO world. Additionally, Lifecore recently announced that much of their pipeline is moving to the right as customer timing has shifted. As a result, the stock has been severely punished.

The shifting timeline is of course frustrating, but I think the market is over-reacting to fears of a potential capital raise as the company's series A convertible preferred shares mature this summer. However, these shares are designed to PIK at maturity if the company is unable to redeem them, so no immediate liquidity needs are on the horizon. As the business the company has already won comes in the door, the company will be well positioned to deal with this and other capital structure needs.

In my view, the gap between the public market value of LFCR stock and the private market value of Lifecore's assets has never been wider. Management deserves full credit for executing at a very high level, but in my view it is incumbent upon the board to close this gap through a sale of the company. I believe (but am not certain) that private buyers were willing to pay approximately $8 per share for Lifecore three years ago, and since that time management has taken out a lot of cost and won a lot of new business.

Snatching Defeat from the Jaws of Victory

During the quarter I exited two long held positions, both of which were large winners at one point, and both of which left the portfolio under less auspicious circumstances. While I did sell some stock along the way, in both cases it seems as if I was too slow to react to changing circumstances and I allowed good investments to turn into mediocre investments. Part of the slow reaction time is explained by my belief that both of these businesses are longer term winners. However, business execution is far from the sole contributor to stock market performance, and as it became clear that the world around these investments was changing or had changed, exiting became the best path forward. I would not be surprised if we owned these businesses again in the future.

PAR Technology Corp (PAR) – PAR was our restaurant software business. I continue to believe that PAR will win their category, but I was never completely comfortable with valuation, which for PAR and other software companies has been measured as a multiple of annual recurring revenue, or "ARR" over the last several years. This concept has gone out of favor due to the rise of AI, and it is not clear to me when it will come back in favor. Consider that during the last major software disruption in the early 2000s companies like Microsoft traded at 12x earnings. As I said, I believe that PAR will win their category, but it will be some time before customer wins translate into real earnings, and with ARR out of favor the downside protection to me in this investment was not clear. Further, there has always been an element of reflexivity tied to PAR's capital structure due to convertible debt. A high stock price allows you to refinance converts with minimal pain, while a low share price brings significant dilution. I exited our position prior to PAR raising a new convert at significantly lower conversion prices than the previous instrument.

Vistry Group PLC (VTY-L) – Vistry was our UK homebuilder that was transitioning away from traditional home building and toward an asset light “partnerships” model focused on low-income housing. As part of this transition, a year ago Vistry announced they had found accounting missteps tied to their legacy business, which led to a selloff in the shares. Nevertheless, I did not sell at that time because the key elements of the thesis were that Vistry had best in class management and would be returning a lot of capital to shareholders via share repurchases. A legacy accounting problem did not weaken the thesis.

However, on their most recent earnings call Vistry announced that CEO Greg Fitzgerald would be retiring, and that they will “continue to consider” future buybacks. Clearly, these developments impaired the thesis. That being said, the structural deficit in affordable housing in the U.K. is still a problem, and massive government funding should still be flowing through the system in the years to come. As capital allocation plans and management succession plans become more clear, Vistry will likely be worth re-visiting.

Looking Forward

As always, I have no idea what to expect in the near term for the stock market, and neither does anyone else. What I remain convinced of however is that our businesses are building value, and as long as they continue to execute, with time we will be rewarded. This is true regardless of what happens with global politics, domestic politics, oil prices, or anything else.

It may also be worth noting that during the first ten years of LWC’s existence, large cap stocks have ruled the day, while small cap stocks – where we play – have been out of favor. Historically this relationship has flip-flopped every 10 or 12 years. Recent market performance seems to suggest that a reversal may be upon us. I am fully aware of the arguments against small cap stocks, including private equity buyers picking over smaller companies and private companies delaying their IPOs. Nevertheless, the gap between large cap and small cap valuations remains near record spreads at a time when small cap earnings are expected to outpace large cap earnings going forward. iii

Of course, nothing is ever guaranteed, and ultimately our performance will be determined by my ability to pick the right investments for our partnerships. But if we were able to outperform large caps during a 10 year stretch when large caps had every advantage over our strategy, I am hopeful that a return to small cap prominence will provide us a significant tailwind over the next 10 years.

Matthew Sweeney

Managing Partner

Appendix: Theravance Biopharma Inc

Theravance Biopharma Inc (TBPH) is a special situation where the downside is well protected by cash on the balance sheet, near term milestone payments, and near-term cash flow, and the upside is likely 50-100% over the next 0-6 months.

Following the failure of a Phase 3 study and a ~30% decline in share price, the company is now ~$400M in cash, $100M in 2026 milestones tied to a sales goal that was easily exceeded in 2025, ~$2.6B in Irish tax assets, and ~$60 -$70M per year in FCF tied to a 35% profit share from sales of YUPELRI, a growing pulmonary drug that treats COPD with patent protection until 2039. YUPELRI is majority owned and commercialized by Viatris (VTRS). The $65M in FCF is pro forma for Theravance following through on already announced plans to reduce headcount and otherwise cut expenses by 60%, which is expected to be complete by Q3'26. Further, the company has announced that the work of their Strategic Review Committee is being “accelerated”. The preceding information can all be found in the 8K that the company released on 3/3/26 following the Phase 3 failure, but to sum it up, the $65M cash flow stream is for sale.

To cut to the chase, valuation can be backed into 2 ways. The first is a simple DCF which is of course sensitive to inputs.

I then discount back by 10%, add expected Q1'26 cash (guided by the company & no debt) of $400M, add $100M in milestone payments due Q1'27, and divide by diluted shares to get around $20 in value. This assumes zero value for the $2.6B in Irish tax assets.

A Brief Word on YUPELRI and COPD

Before defending these inputs, first a bit about YUPELRI. YUPELRI is a Long-acting Muscarinic Antagonist (LAMA). LAMAs are used to treat Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), and act as bronchodilators, meaning they help keep the airways open for an extended period (about 24 hours). YUPELRI is the only once-daily, nebulized LAMA maintenance medicine for COPD. Theravance discovered and developed revefenacin, which is the API in YUPELRI, and partnered with Mylan (which became Viatris after merging with Pfizer’s generics unit in 2020) in 2015 to develop and commercialize the drug. Viatris now leads commercialization and marketing, while Theravance co-promotes, focusing on the hospital/institutional channel in the U.S. 65% of domestic net sales go to Viatris, and 35% go to Theravance. Viatris also handles commercialization and marketing in the rest of the world, while Theravance can receive tiered royalties on sales in ROW.

LAMAs work by blocking muscarinic receptors in the smooth muscle of the airways, and are considered first-line maintenance therapy for moderate to severe COPD. LAMAs are typically just part of a treatment plan, with many patients being prescribed both a LAMA and a Long-Acting Beta Agonist (LABA) which work through a different receptor pathway than LAMAs. Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS) are also commonly used as well, and LAMA + LABA + ICS is known as triple therapy.

There are of course new potential treatment options being developed for COPD, but bronchodilators like LAMAs are expected to remain as the backbone of any treatment plan, and are unlikely to be disrupted any time in the foreseeable future.

Supporting Assumptions

Growth: YUPELRI sales grew 12% in 2025, the drug has about 5% share in a domestic market that is expected to grow 4-5% per year and was recently approved in China. Theravance will receive 14% of China sales under $75M, 17% of sales between $75M and $150M, and 20% of sales above $150M, as well as certain milestones along the way. Domestically, the company investor presentation lays out their strategy for growth (which appears to be working), but in brief according to Theravance many COPD patients are inappropriately using short-acting nebulizers 4-6x per day rather than a LAMA once a day, and many patients are relying on handheld inhalers throughout the day rather than the once daily LAMA. To be fair, this is a competitive space, which is why I am not more aggressive with growth assumptions, but it seems reasonable to think that the only once a day treatment will at least grow in line with the market -if not take share as it has been recently -over the next few years.

Discount Rate: This is all obviously finger in the air, but I start with 10% based on general commentary from industry participants, as well as this document titled “ Pharmaceutical Royalty Structures: A Global Technical Analysis. ” If you take the discount to 12%, value drops to around $18.50. At 15%, value drops to a bit below $17. The reality is that you can make a DCF say whatever you want. The point here though is that even if you use 15%, the outcome from an investment in equity at today’s prices is pretty good, especially given the likely short timeline. At 10%, the outcome is great, and it is not crazy to think that a majority partner would pay a 10% rate to lock up the asset (more on this below). It is also not crazy to think they’d discount at 8% given their familiarity with the drug and its prospects, and that gets you north of $21.

Patent Protection: There is a Composition of Matter patent that expires in 2028, a Polymorph Patent that expires in 2031, and a Method of Use patent that expires in 2039. In brief, this means that after 2028 competitors could sell generics for other uses but cannot promote it for the same use case as YUPELRI, which is maintenance treatment of COPD.

Of note, in 2022 multiple generic makers filed ANDAs, and the company has settled with all 8 of them, ensuring they will not be entering the market until 2039.

Expected milestones: Theravance previously sold their TRELEGY royalty to Royalty Pharma in 2022 while retaining the rights to outer year royalties. The outer year rights were sold to GSK (GSK) in 2025. The sales to Royalty Pharma included milestone payments which Theravance has been collecting. The most recent payment was $50M in February 2026 for exceeding 2025 sales goals with net sales of $3.91B. The FY2026 net sales goal is $3.51B, which will trigger an additional $100M royalty payment. As the 2026 goal was easily exceeded in 2025, the company considers collection of this $100M highly likely.

Additional milestone payments of up to $45M are possible depending on YUPELRI sales in China, which is the world’s largest COPD market. I give zero credit for these potential milestones.

A Second Approach to Valuation

The other way that I think about valuation is through the eyes of Viatris. Again, Viatris is the majority partner here, keeping 65% of net sales. This is a $16.5B market cap company with 1,400 different molecules across 7,500 products for sale, and a $1B buyback in place. Viatris is guiding to $2.15B in 2026 FCF. In other words, Viatris seems willing to pay at least 7.7x FCF for their own stock. As YUPELRI is growing faster than the system average and has patent protection length on par with the system average, it seems likely that Viatris would be better served by paying 7.7x for Theravance’s cut of the YUPELRI sales than they would by buying their own stock at 7.7x. If they were to pay 7.7x, Theravance would be worth approximately $19 per share.

Again I give zero credit for the Irish tax assets, and do not boost the FCF to reflect synergies that Viatris could surely recognize by eliminating the Theravance sales force and just putting YUPELRI in their existing hospital reps’ bags or having their YUPELRI field reps cover hospitals as well. Even if Viatris determined they needed to keep the reps, they could surely take cost out on the administrative side of the sales process.

How Do We Know Management Will Follow Through?

As mentioned, the company 8K'd that they will be "accelerating" the work of the company's Strategic Review Committee. This committee is the result of pressure from 2 activists that have been involved for a few years now: Irenic Capital and Weiss Asset Management. Both Irenic and Weiss have board representation and have been pushing for the company to realize value rather than fund science projects. They had previous success with Theravance's sale of TRELEGY, which is the source of the $100M milestone payment that should arrive in Q1'27. It may also be worth noting that the director who is running the strategic review committee is also the former CFO of Royalty Pharma (RPRX). RPRX could also be a potential buyer here, although that may necessitate a creative solution to the hospital sales force. In any case, 2 activists that have already had success in the boardroom have been steering the company in the direction of a sale for several years now. It is extremely likely that they follow through.

Why Won't Viatris Just Low Ball? / Other Potential Buyers?

Theravance's 35% cut of YUPELRI is a bit of an odd duck, as it is NOT a pure play royalty stream. As mentioned previously, Theravance is responsible for maintaining a hospital focused sales force to peddle YUPELRI in the hospital channel. This wrinkle could understandably prevent a purely financial buyer from being interested in the cash flow stream.

It seems obvious that Viatris is the most logical buyer – they already own 65%, they know the market, and they know the drug. However, because they are such a logical buyer there is a possibility that Viatris will only come with a low-ball bid.

The recipe to prevent a low-ball bid is of course other potential buyers. So who else might be interested?

The answer is basically anyone who is also involved in the other pieces of the "triple therapy" described above. Following the planned restructuring, TBPH will still have ~$40M of SG&A costs which supports a sales force, a sales support team, a home office, a management team, etc. etc. For any company that is already calling on pulmonologists as part of the COPD triple therapy world (or for other reasons), they could likely wipe out almost all of these costs (assuming they already have salespeople focused on the hospital/institutional channel), and simply put YUPELRI into their existing pulmonary sales forces' bag.

You can capitalize or account for these savings however you want – simply adding back another $30M of annual expense to the FCF in the DCF above will get you a value north of $24 per share. But from my perspective, it is less important to try to fully value these costs and generate an upside

case than it is to simply think that Viatris should be motivated to come in with a legitimate bid rather than a low-ball bid. Surely they don't want 35% of their profits in the hands of a competitor.

About Those Irish Tax Assets....

I am not an Irish (or domestic) tax expert. What seems clear though is that in order to be able to use the tax assets, any buyer would have to continue on in the same business line that generated the tax assets. In other words, if a buyer were simply after the YUPELRI cash flows and did not also have an R&D arm, they would likely not be able to use the assets. Most important in my mind is the fact that Viatris does in fact have a significant R&D presence in Ireland across 5 sites that employ 1,600 people. It follows then that Viatris would ascribe some value to these tax assets. Again, this is a finger in the air exercise, but if you figure that these tax assets are worth 6.25% of face (half the Irish tax rate), that gets you to around $23 based on the above valuations. Or you can assume that the 10% discount rate in the DCF above is too aggressive, and assume that a 15% discount rate would be more appropriate and still come out with a combined value of somewhere around $20.

In Conclusion

We paid an average price of $13.70 per share for our investment in Theravance. This is an investment where the downside is protected by ~$10/share of cash and near-term milestone payments, and the market is putting a ~3.5x multiple on a quasi-royalty stream that is patent protected for the next 13 years. Importantly, properly incentivized activists are in the board room and pushing for the upside, which is comfortably ~50% and as much as 100% if a buyer thinks they can take all of the cost out by dropping YUPELRI in their existing sales forces' bags and realize value from the Irish tax assets. As a Strategic Review Committee has recently "accelerated" their work and the most likely buyer would have negligible diligence requirements, it seems likely that this situation will be resolved within the next 0 to 6 months.
